What do you think? … [...] Patrick Krekelberg

Local-first writing is still underrated for unfinished thinking. Draft notes, specs, prompts, and private business planning often need to exist before they are polished enough for a cloud workspace; offline ownership changes the way people are willing to write honestly.

Etienne Garcia [...] Roopesh Donde

Loved the aesthetics. I like how instantly it makes me focussed and think on what to write.   
Very minimalist and no distractions.   
One thing I would suggest to make tying sound off on default. Its Novel to hear the sounds at first, but it quickly becomes a distraction and doubles up with the actual keyboard sounds.

Tina Chhabra

there's something nice about a writing tool that just opens and works. no login, no sync setup, no onboarding flow. the ambient writing modes are a cool touch too. would be great to see some kind of simple export to markdown file so you can move notes into other tools when you need to.

Patrick Krekelberg

results.title: I replaced Notion and Evernote with an offline-first system, and it's a ...
results.content:
By default, Joplin is local-first. All notes live on your device and are fully accessible offline. Syncing is optional and can be handled through services like Nextcloud, Dropbox, or a self-hosted WebDAV server. Joplin also offers end-to-end encryption so your data is encrypted before it ever leaves your device. You’re in complete control of how and where your notes are stored, and how they move between devices, without being forced into a single cloud provider. [...] pasting NotebookLM response in Joplin

Joplin is an open-source, local-first note-taking app that works as a solid replacement for Evernote, especially for longer, text-heavy notes. It’s built on Markdown, which keeps your notes portable and future-proof, and therefore much easier to manage and access over time, regardless of your toolkit. This also means there’s no proprietary format: Joplin stores everything in plain text. The interface is simple and very utilitarian, and notes are organized into “notebooks” rather than folders, but with a similar hierarchical structure. [...] The app works best for quick captures like small snippets of text, lists, meeting notes, and things of that nature. And it uses plain text with support for Markdown formatting. Another cool thing is that you can actually import your data from Google Keep, Apple Notes, or Standard Notes, so you don’t have to start from scratch in Unforget. It also lets you export all your notes in JSON format.

### Building a local-first toolkit

results.title: Local-first software: You own your data, in spite of the cloud
results.content:
Git and GitHub are primarily used by software engineers to collaborate on source code. They are perhaps the closest thing we have to a true local-first software package: compared to server-centric version control systems such as Subversion, Git works fully offline, it is fast, it gives full control to users, and it is suitable for long-term preservation of data. This is the case because a Git repository on your local filesystem is a primary copy of the data, and is not subordinate to any server. [...] However, on mobile platforms (iOS and Android), Dropbox and its cousins use a completely different model. The mobile apps do not synchronize an entire folder — instead, they are thin clients that fetch your data from a server one file at a time, and by default they do not work offline. There is a “Make available offline” option, but you need to remember to invoke it ahead of going offline, it is clumsy, and only works when the app is open. The Dropbox API is also very server-centric.

 

Users of the Dropbox mobile app spend a lot of time looking at spinners, a stark contrast to the at-your-fingertips feeling of the Dropbox desktop product. [...] As these tools use the local filesystem, they have many attractive properties: access to local files is fast, and working offline is no problem (files edited offline are synced the next time an Internet connection is available). If the sync service were shut down, your files would still remain unharmed on your local disk, and it would be easy to switch to a different syncing service. If your computer’s hard drive fails, you can restore your work simply by installing the app and waiting for it to sync. This provides good longevity and control over your data.

results.title: Privacy-First Writing: Why Your Manuscripts Deserve Local ...
results.content:
Your files live on your computer — Not on a remote server
 The software works offline — No internet required for core functionality
 You control backups — Back up to your own drives, cloud storage you choose, or both
 Data stays local by default — Network features are opt-in, not the default
 No dependency on service availability — The software works even if the company disappears

Local-first doesn’t mean anti-cloud. It means cloud features are optional additions, not requirements.

## How Local-First Writing Software Works

Section titled “How Local-First Writing Software Works”

### Storage

Section titled “Storage” [...] ### AI Without Exposure

Section titled “AI Without Exposure”

Some local-first tools offer AI features while maintaining privacy. The approach varies:

 Noveling: AI features are optional and only activated when you choose. The editor and all creative tools work completely offline. When you use AI, the specific text you select is sent to AI providers — not your entire manuscript. You choose which AI model to use and when.
 Other tools: Some use on-device AI models that never send data to external servers, though the quality is typically lower than cloud-based models.

The privacy advantage: AI works on the specific text you choose, when you choose, rather than having your entire manuscript continuously accessible to AI services.

## Comparing Security Approaches

results.title: Writing Offline with Writebox - Life Untethered
results.content: So what is a writer to do when she has abandoned all things Office and cannot rely on a strong Internet connection? There are many alternatives to writing offline, including Google, but mostly they come in app form. The closest thing that I have found to writing in a don’t-think-just write, old school, pen-and-paper style is Writebox by Writebox Apps. Quoting from the creator of Writebox, I like this app because “Writebox is very simple and allows you to concentrate on your writing.” It’s kind of like saying that hiking is simple, because it allows you to be one with nature; or that meditation is simple, because you can be one with the Divine; or that tiny living is simple, because you can concentrate on more important things. This is not to say that writing isn’t easy.I just said that [...] This is not to say that writing isn’t easy.I just said that Writebox makes the task of writing offline simple. [...] However, I am a writer, and I do write, and I currently write for Life Untethered. When the stars are aligned and I can carve out time to express myself, often my abilities to let it flow are impeded by lack of WiFi. If you remember, I use a Toshiba Chromebook 2, which works almost exclusively in the Cloud, so WiFi is necessary to do almost anything on it and it stores very little locally. Obviously, as a Chromebook user, I utilize the Chrome browser and Google services as well, but I have found Google’s offline writing option to be incredibly difficult to work with, and, inevitably, a huge distraction. Furthermore, we use WordPress for our work. WordPress has a “distraction free” writing option, but not an offline one. Also, every once in a while, WordPress likes to kick me off, most
